facts.text: Hamerton is the only person who knows enough about his own history to give a truthful account of it.
Hamerton dreaded the possibility of falling into the hands of biographers with inadequate materials.
Hamerton had already been selected as a subject by two or three biographers with friendly intentions.
Hamerton believed that an autobiography can be more truthful than a biography written by someone with slight acquaintance.
Hamerton held that readers have a fair claim to hear the truth in a biography.
Hamerton's rule was to say nothing that could hurt the living.
Hamerton intended to deal with the memory of the dead as tenderly as possible while maintaining truthfulness.
Hamerton had kind feelings towards the dead because he expected to be dead when his autobiography was published.
Hamerton found the notion of being a dead man not entirely displeasing.
Hamerton felt a new kind of security writing his autobiography knowing it would not be published until after his death.
Hamerton had been contemplating death for at least an hour before writing this chapter.
Hamerton was born at Laneside near Shaw in Lancashire in 1834.
Hamerton's maternal grandfather owned Laneside, a small estate with houses and a cotton-mill.
Hamerton's grandfather Cocker was a lively and vigorous man who enjoyed life heartily.
Hamerton's grandmother was descended from the De Cromptons of Crompton Hall.
Hamerton's grandmother had a great appreciation of the value of money.
Hamerton's father came to Shaw as a young attorney.
Hamerton's father had a fine person, great physical strength, and fifteen quarterings.
Hamerton's father had a reputation for dissolute habits.
Hamerton's father was a good horseman and an excellent shot.
Hamerton's father looked very well in a ball-room.
Hamerton's father had an unhappy faculty for shining in masculine company.
Hamerton's father had little money except what he earned in his profession.
Hamerton's mother was a young lady with a will of her own and a sweet and amiable disposition.
Hamerton's mother publicly declared her intentions to marry his father by dancing with him at a charity ball in Shaw.
Hamerton's grandmother initially opposed the match between his parents.
Hamerton's parents married without his grandmother's consent in June 1833.
Hamerton's father wrote a formal letter to his future mother-in-law requesting her consent to the marriage.
Hamerton's father promised that his wife's property would be settled upon herself so he could have no control over it.
Hamerton's father intended to reform his way of life through marriage.
Hamerton's parents' marriage was immensely happy during the short time it lasted.
Hamerton's mother died prematurely, little more than one year after her marriage.
Hamerton was born on September 10, 1834.
Hamerton's mother died on September 27, 1834, at twenty minutes past eleven at night.
Hamerton's mother died of mere weakness without any absolute disease, according to three doctors.
Hamerton's mother had been very delicate for a long time.
Hamerton's father was twenty-nine years old when his wife died.
Hamerton's mother had blue eyes, a very fair complexion, and rich auburn hair.
Hamerton has never had any symptoms of consumption in the fifty-one years since his mother's death.
No portrait of Hamerton's mother was ever taken.
Hamerton once played the part of a young lady in a charade and was told he resembled his mother.
Hamerton's father customarily wrote the family name with a double 'm'.
Hamerton's mother died when he was very young.
Hamerton was uncertain whether his mother died of consumption or debility.
Hamerton has never shown symptoms of consumption.
Hamerton's mother had blue eyes, fair complexion, and auburn hair.
Hamerton's mother was physically active and a good horsewoman.
Hamerton's mother went shooting with his father on one occasion and killed something, then cried over her victim.
Hamerton's mother was twenty-four years old when she died.
Hamerton has lived more than twice as long as his mother.
Hamerton's father never remarried after his mother's death.
Hamerton's father lost interest in his profession after his mother's death.
Hamerton's father received a testimonial from a client in 1834 for his legal work.
Hamerton's father became indolent and indifferent after inheriting his wife's property.
Hamerton's father sought relief from depression through the society of drinking companions.
Hamerton's father had great physical strength and could drink heavily without showing obvious effects.
Hamerton's father was considered the cleverest member of his family.
Hamerton's father had no intellectual tastes and rarely purchased books.
Hamerton's father's book collection included a biography of William IV, sketches of Reform Ministers, Baines's History of Lancashire, and a Geographical Dictionary.
Hamerton's father did not own books by popular English poets of his time or novels by Sir Walter Scott.
Hamerton's father read the Times newspaper and its leading articles.
Hamerton's father was indifferent to the fine arts.
Hamerton's father drank brandy in hot strong grogs with unlimited tobacco.
Hamerton's father dined in the middle of the day and drank through the afternoon and evening.
Hamerton's father drank ale in the morning to slake his thirst.
Hamerton's father separated himself from his own social class.
Hamerton's father had a constitution stronger than most men.
Hamerton's father was completely independent and free to travel or advance his profession.
Hamerton was not brought up under his father's roof during childhood.
Hamerton was sent to live with his father's two unmarried sisters during childhood.
Hamerton's aunts lived in Burnley with their mother.
Burnley was a small country town in an aristocratic neighborhood during Hamerton's childhood.
Hamerton's grandfather, Gilbert Hamerton, was the second son of an ancient Yorkshire family.
Hamerton's grandfather possessed an old gabled mansion with a small estate near Towneley Park.
Hamerton's grandfather married a farmer's daughter named Jane Smith.
Hamerton's grandmother was a farmer's daughter who became an excellent wife and mother.
Hamerton's grandmother was well-respected in society despite marrying above her station.
Hamerton's grandmother was the first dead human body Hamerton ever saw.
Hamerton's grandfather and his brother were completely and permanently estranged.
Hamerton's grandfather's brother was the squire of Hellifield Peel.
Hamerton's grandfather lived at Hollins, inherited from his mother.
Hamerton's grandfather's brother lived in a castellated peel tower at Hellifield built in 1440.
The two family estates were only about twenty miles apart.
Hamerton's grandfather and his brother never met after their quarrel.
Hamerton's grandfather lived in apparently excellent health until age seventy-seven.
Hamerton's grandfather died suddenly while seated in his dining-room at Hollins.
Hamerton's grandfather asked his daughter to open a window and say a prayer before dying.
Hamerton's grandfather preserved the costume and manners of the eighteenth century, wearing a pig-tail, breeches, and shoe-buckles.
Hamerton's grandfather had a great liking for the French language.
Hamerton's grandfather took life too easily for any intellectual achievements.
Hamerton remembers his grandmother as an old lady always dressed in black.
Hamerton had intense affection for his grandmother and remembers her kind words.
Hamerton felt vague pity for his loss as a child because people looked at him with tenderness for his mother's sake.
Hamerton later understood that his mother's death was a sacrifice that allowed him to live.
Hamerton's grandfather died with his daughter present forty miles away, and his grandson reported seeing his grandfather's apparition.
Hamerton's grandfather wore eighteenth-century costume including a pig-tail, breeches, and shoe-buckles.
Hamerton's grandfather had a great liking for the French language and wrote an original French grammar.
Hamerton's grandfather printed his French grammar in synoptic sheets at his private expense, though it was never completed or published.
Hamerton believed he may have inherited his aptitude for French from his grandfather.
Hamerton's grandfather never lived in France and never visited the country.
Hamerton's grandfather's French conversations were chiefly held with a Roman Catholic chaplain at Towneley Hall.
Hamerton's grandfather traveled in Portugal for six months.
Hamerton's grandfather's traveling companion, a younger brother, died abroad from sleeping in a damp bed.
During Hamerton's grandfather's absence, an estate agent cut down the old avenue of oaks leading to the house.
Hamerton confessed himself to be a lover of trees, avenues, and old houses.
Hamerton's grandfather brought up all three of his sons to be solicitors as the readiest means of earning a living.
Hamerton's eldest uncle heartily disliked the legal profession.
Hamerton's other uncle regarded the legal profession with cool indifference.
Hamerton's father expressed his desire that Hamerton should never be a lawyer.
Hamerton's father believed a man had enough to plague him in his own concerns without troubling his mind about those of other people.
Hamerton's aunts remained aristocratic in their feelings throughout their lives.
Hamerton's aunts liked to enjoy the hospitality of great houses in the neighborhood.
Hamerton's uncles and father abandoned all aristocratic memories and aspirations and entered frankly into the middle class.
Hamerton believed his aunts showed better taste in liking refined society than his father did in lowering himself to associate with men of inferior stamp.
One of Hamerton's aunts preferred gentlefolks because their manners were more agreeable.
Hamerton's aunts gave him his beginning of education.
Hamerton learned to read English with the greatest facility.
Hamerton was sent to Grammar School at Burnley at the age of five and a half.
Hamerton's schoolmaster considered him so well forward that he was set at once to Latin.
Hamerton was made to learn Latin out of a grammar written in that language.
Hamerton retained recollection of the perfectly useless mental fatigue and puzzlement from learning abstract grammatical statements in Latin.
Hamerton believed the idea of making a child five and a half years old learn a dead language by abstract rules is a great error.
Hamerton's progress in Latin was very slow.
Hamerton developed a horror of everything printed in Latin that he did not overcome for many years.
Hamerton's schoolmaster had a rude disposition and sometimes amused himself by putting Hamerton and another child on his knees and bringing their heads into collision.
Hamerton remembered the sensation of being stunned by the schoolmaster's collisions but was not aware his Latin improved from it.
Hamerton's recollection of his early years at school is extremely vague.
Hamerton was taken once or twice a year to his father and always disliked and dreaded those visits.
Hamerton feared his father greatly and with good reason.
As a child, Hamerton persuaded his father's groom to let him mount a gray saddle-horse.
Hamerton was given a whip and left the bridle to himself on the horse, applied the whip vigorously, and was thrown off and carried back covered with blood.
Hamerton's father employed a tailor who made Hamerton a suit without particulars, creating a costume that was an exact copy of a full-grown squire's dress on a small scale.
Hamerton's suit consisted of a green cut-away coat, a yellow waistcoat, and green trousers adorned with gilt buttons.
Hamerton was proud of his suit and did not understand the laughter it provoked.
Hamerton's father tyrannically decreed that Hamerton was never to wear the suit.
Hamerton was capable of regretting the suit even into adulthood.
Hamerton undertook a tour in Wales with his father and his father's eldest sister in the summer of 1842.
Hamerton's aunt made him keep a journal of the Wales tour, which he still possessed.
Hamerton was accustomed to live with grown-up people and had no companions of his own age in the same house.
Hamerton had cousins at Hollins and friends at school.
Hamerton acquired a way of talking about things as older people talk.
Hamerton was very observant and received very strong impressions, especially from buildings such as old castles and cathedrals.
Hamerton had a topographic habit of mind even in childhood that made every fresh locality interesting and engraved it on his memory.
Hamerton's journal was written as an exercise to please his father.
Hamerton had been learning Latin for more than two years when he wrote the Wales journal at age eight.
Hamerton had a great interest in all beautiful materials throughout the pages of his journal.
Hamerton noticed the kind of wood used for suites of furniture and invariably mentioned it in his journal.
Hamerton had a special instinct for appreciating beautiful materials, which he described as a kind of sympathy with Nature.
Hamerton's interest in materials has always been very strong.
Hamerton's interest in materials is both artistic and utilitarian.
All metals, woods, marble, etc., are delightful to Hamerton in some way.
In 1842 Rhyl was a little quiet place known to Liverpool people as a good bathing-place.
Hamerton urged his father to buy a genteel cottage in Rhyl because it pleased him on account of possibilities for boating and riding on the sands.
Hamerton had enjoyed excellent fishing during the Wales tour, which delighted him as a child, though he gave up the amusement afterwards.
Hamerton remembered the Rhyl house very distinctly as his father's last chance of escape from his habits and associates.
During the Wales tour, Hamerton's father conducted himself as a man ought to do who is travelling with a lady and a child.
Hamerton's father was not harsh with him during the Wales tour, and some of those Welsh days were pleasant for Hamerton to live over again in memory.
Hamerton's father refused to buy the Rhyl house because it did not seem a good investment, being a flimsy little building not very well contrived.
Hamerton's father bought him a little bay mare at Rhyl that was a pretty and swift creature.
The bay mare was pitched into the sea from a steamer at Menai and made to swim ashore.
The mare became broken-winded after being in a hot place on the steamer near the engines and then suddenly exposed to cold sea-water.
Hamerton was greatly grieved by the mare becoming broken-winded.
Hamerton had very tender feelings about animals down to the present day.
Hamerton visited Anglesea and Caernarvon during the Wales tour.
Hamerton's father refused to buy a house because it seemed like a poor investment.
Hamerton's father bought him a little bay mare at Rhyl.
The bay mare became broken-winded after being thrown into the sea during transport.
Hamerton had tender feelings about animals throughout his life.
Hamerton was struck by the beauty of the Menai suspension bridge rather than its size or cost.
The Welsh tour in 1842 was the last year Hamerton could live in happy ignorance of evil.
Hamerton's father was extremely severe and often more harsh than the occasion warranted.
Hamerton's father's severity was partly due to his habit of drinking brandy.
Hamerton had a well-founded dread of his father's presence when inflamed with alcohol.
Hamerton's father was very uncertain in his moods and greetings.
One of Hamerton's aunts always accompanied him during visits to his father for protection.
Hamerton feared being left alone with his father.
In June 1843, Hamerton's father ordered him to come live under his roof permanently.
Hamerton had feelings toward his elder aunt that were like those of a loving son toward his mother.
Hamerton's elder aunt had completely taken the place of his mother.
Hamerton's father owned Ivy Cottage in Shaw.
Hamerton's father kept saddle horses and ponies but no carriage.
Hamerton's father was exacting about horse grooming and equipment.
Hamerton's father rode a jet-black mare of vicious temper.
Hamerton's pony was pure white with a full mane and tail.
Hamerton rode in constant dread of his father's hunting whip with a steel hammer at the end.
Hamerton's father struck him with the whip hammer when he turned his toes out.
Hamerton's father became angry when Hamerton showed gentleness or tenderness.
Hamerton loved his pony Lily and did not like to beat her.
Hamerton had a young dog that was very dear to him.
Hamerton's father kicked Hamerton's dog, causing it to die.
Hamerton's father frequently stopped at inns during rides to drink.
Hamerton's father once fell from his horse on a paved street but was caught by bystanders.
Hamerton's father fell with his foot caught in the stirrup and was dragged down a steep hill.
Hamerton's father wore a top-coat that protected him during the dragging accident.
Hamerton's father sent him on errands with strict time limits.
Hamerton's father believed in applying the mind completely to one thing at a time.
Hamerton's father hated desultory effort and busy idleness.
Hamerton's father swore freely but punished Hamerton for swearing.
Hamerton's father hated lies and the shadow of lies above all things.
Hamerton's father's precept 'Tell the truth and shame the devil' sank deep into Hamerton's nature.
Hamerton became genuinely difficult for him to tell lies due to his father's teaching.
Hamerton was punished with two hours of dumb-bell drill for allegedly lying about his exercise time.
Hamerton's father punished him for explaining that he pulled paper rather than tore it.
Hamerton's father taught him riding well.
Hamerton's father made him take cold shower-baths to accustom him to water.
Hamerton's father attached great importance to arithmetic, reading aloud, and intelligible writing.
Hamerton's father had limited education and knew no Greek and only minimal Latin.
Hamerton's father desired Hamerton to be well taught despite his own limited education.
Hamerton's father placed gold and silver in his keeping to teach him about money.
Hamerton never lost or misapplied any money his father entrusted to him.
Hamerton was sent to a village school near his father's house to practice calligraphy and arithmetic.
Hamerton fell in love with a girl about a year older than himself at the village school.
The girl Hamerton loved squinted to an unfortunate degree.
Hamerton's existence at Ivy Cottage was one of extreme dullness varied by dread.
Every meal at Ivy Cottage was a tête-à-tête with his father with no other company.
Hamerton's father became increasingly gloomy as his nervous system gave way.
Hamerton was sent to school to practice calligraphy and arithmetic.
Hamerton fell in love with a girl about a year older than himself while at school.
Hamerton attended school with both boys and girls in the same schoolroom.
Hamerton had meals alone with his father, unrelieved by the presence of any lady or young person.
Hamerton found it a relief when guests came to dinner.
Hamerton once believed a dinner guest was the Duke of Wellington.
Hamerton's brain received images of surrounding creatures and things with unnatural clearness and intensity during his time of anxiety.
Hamerton remembered details of Ivy Cottage vividly forty years later.
Hamerton meditated flight from his father one night while sitting by his bedroom window.
Hamerton's father's cruelty had reached its highest point before Hamerton considered fleeing.
Hamerton was frequently beaten by his father.
Hamerton's meals with his father had become intolerable.
Hamerton had sovereigns in a drawer but refused to use them for escape because he considered it a breach of trust.
Hamerton decided against fleeing because he feared his uncles would not believe his story and would send him back.
Hamerton feared his father would disinherit him if he attempted to flee.
Hamerton's father had another heir in view whom Hamerton never saw.
Hamerton's father attempted to throw himself out a window during a week of delirium.
It took six men to hold Hamerton's father on his bed during his attack.
Hamerton's aunt Mary came to stay during his father's illness.
Hamerton loved his aunt Mary with filial tenderness and gratitude.
Hamerton's six-month ordeal at Ivy Cottage seemed longer than any half-dozen years before or after.
Hamerton's father ordered him to read the Times aloud during his recovery.
Hamerton's father accused him of inserting words into the Times article that were not there.
Hamerton had been habituated by his father's precept to be truthful even to him.
Hamerton's aunt listened at the door during the Times incident, fearing for his safety.
Hamerton's father had a fit of apoplexy and died the next morning.
Hamerton was not in the room when his father died.
Hamerton was deeply affected by his father's death despite the cruelty he suffered.
Hamerton mourned the father who had been kind to him in Wales rather than the cruel master at Ivy Cottage.
Hamerton's father was thirty-nine years old at his death.
Hamerton's father had a complete set of perfectly sound teeth at the time of his death.
Hamerton's father was buried in the same tomb as Hamerton's mother.
Hamerton's father's funeral took place in January 1844.
Hamerton developed a permanent dislike to Shaw and its neighborhood as a consequence of his life at Ivy Cottage.
Hamerton never went to Shaw again quite willingly after his father's death.
Hamerton's grandmother lived in the village after his father's death.
Hamerton was taken to see his grandmother every year until her death.
Hamerton lay awake at his grandmother's house realizing the horrible nights he had passed at Ivy Cottage.
Hamerton avoids whatever reminds him of his time at Ivy Cottage even forty years later.
Hamerton's father named his eldest sister Mary as Hamerton's sole guardian.
Hamerton's father appointed two lawyers as co-executors with Mary.
Hamerton's father had no great affection for his brothers.
Hamerton's uncles were always kind to him but held completely aloof from paternal interference with his education.
Hamerton's aunt Mary was appointed as his guardian despite being an old maid.
Hamerton attended Doncaster School after his father's death.
Hamerton had a feeling for architecture and music.
Hamerton's guardian insisted on his learning French.
Hamerton began to learn the violin.
Hamerton had an aversion to cricket.
Hamerton loved reading Scott.
Hamerton had his first library during his school years.
Hamerton studied classical subjects at school.
Hamerton had extremely painful memories of nights spent at Ivy Cottage with his father.
Hamerton's father appointed his eldest sister Mary as Hamerton's sole guardian.
Hamerton's father named two lawyers as co-executors with Mary.
Hamerton's aunt Mary was a woman of excellent sense.
Hamerton's aunt encouraged him in manly pursuits rather than raising him effeminately.
Hamerton's aunt was willing to endure anxiety about his safety to allow him to participate in perilous youth activities.
Hamerton's aunt planned for him to attend good schools, then be prepared for Oxford by tutors, and become a clergyman.
Hamerton never felt any desire to rise in the world by making acquaintance with people of rank.
Hamerton never had any worldly ambition in his life.
Hamerton found it hard to be separated from his guardian for five months at a time at Doncaster School.
Hamerton's guardian was always gentle, kind, and considerate.
Hamerton's guardian had remarkable firmness of character and would act against her own feelings and his if duty required it.
Hamerton was sent to Doncaster School in an open hired carriage in August with his guardian and the driver.
Hamerton cried in his dormitory bed on his first night at Doncaster School.
Hamerton found the usher at Doncaster to be vulgar and overbearing but a valuable master.
Hamerton recognized the difference between a master who was a gentleman and one who was not.
Hamerton made very rapid progress at Doncaster chiefly thanks to the usher's help.
Hamerton was once unjustly thrashed by the usher with a switch from an apple-tree without explanation.
Hamerton suspected the usher thrashed him simply to exercise his arm.
Hamerton had inherited prejudice from his Tory family that Dissent was something low and beneath a gentleman's attention.
Hamerton came to the conclusion that the usher was wrong about religious toleration.
Hamerton had intense delight in architecture even before his teens.
Hamerton deeply enjoyed Doncaster old church, which he considered one of the finest buildings of its class in England.
Hamerton was fully alive to the influence of music and architecture in combination.
Hamerton believed music makes architecture alive.
Hamerton felt that sculptured heads between arches gained noble animation from music.
Hamerton's habit of attending service in a beautiful church was a powerful stimulus to his inborn passion for architecture.
Hamerton had already taken drawing lessons before attending Doncaster School.
Hamerton's drawing-master at Doncaster was an elderly, stout, respectable man.
Hamerton's drawing-master had little sympathy and little enthusiasm for the fine arts.
Hamerton's drawing-master promoted him to water-color painting.
Hamerton made the drawing-master angry twice during his lessons.
Hamerton found drawing lessons to be a delightful break in his week.
Hamerton enjoyed the walk to the drawing-master's house twice a week.
Hamerton sketched from nature in the fields around Doncaster with his drawing-master.
Hamerton's guardian insisted he learn French.
Hamerton's guardian had taught him the elements of French but did not speak it.
Hamerton wanted to be excused from French at Doncaster due to the seriousness of Latin and Greek.
Hamerton's guardian refused to allow him to abandon French and wrote determinedly to him and Mr. Cape about it.
Hamerton believed his guardian's insistence on French may have greatly influenced his future life.
Hamerton's French master at Doncaster was an Italian named Testa from a good family.
Hamerton considered Signor Testa one of the most perfect gentlemen he ever met.
Hamerton regretted not learning Italian with Signor Testa.
Hamerton learned Italian later in life with an inferior master.
Hamerton understood that Signor Testa had left Italy due to some political difficulty.
Hamerton knew Signor Testa still had relations in Italy.
Hamerton was moved when Signor Testa showed tears over leaves and a flower from his father's place in Italy.
Hamerton felt cut to the quick by Signor Testa's hurt expression when the class laughed.
Hamerton longed to explain to Signor Testa that the laughter was not directed at him.
Hamerton recognized that Signor Testa had no sense of humor.
Hamerton's respect for Signor Testa increased after the incident with the laughter.
Hamerton tried to please Signor Testa after the incident.
Hamerton found French lessons very agreeable.
Hamerton read French on his own account beyond his lessons.
Hamerton acquired a liking for the French language that remained with him ever since.
Hamerton received instruction in drawing and French, which some considered idle accomplishments.
Hamerton's education included music instruction.
Hamerton had a guardian who made decisions about his education.
Hamerton took French lessons and acquired a lasting liking for the language.
Hamerton studied drawing and water-colors as a boy.
Hamerton attended a mathematical school where boys were prepared for Cambridge.
Hamerton attended Doncaster School.
Hamerton's guardian was Mr. Cape.
Hamerton wrote a persuasive letter to his guardian asking to study the violin.
Hamerton was studying three languages and water-color painting simultaneously.
Hamerton was restricted from practicing violin by himself initially.
Hamerton was a brilliant dancer as a boy despite later disliking dancing intensely.
Hamerton danced a sailor's hornpipe in public at a school theatrical entertainment.
Hamerton acquired an intense aversion to cricket at school.
Hamerton disliked all games except chess and billiards.
Hamerton disliked being compelled to field in cricket to please elder boys.
Hamerton escaped cricket to read Scott's poems in a quiet place near the Red House.
Hamerton possessed Scott's poems in pocket volumes that he still owned as an adult.
Scott's influence was the strongest influence on Hamerton during his school years.
Hamerton began reading Scott with 'The Lady of the Lake.'
Hamerton read 'Marmion,' 'The Lay of the Last Minstrel,' the Ballads, and 'Rokeby' by Scott.
Hamerton collected pocket-sized volumes of Goldsmith, Crabbe, Kirke White, and Moore's 'Irish Melodies.'
Hamerton received 'Paradise Lost' as a school prize for history.
Hamerton's guardian authorized the purchase of Shakespeare in seven pocket volumes during holidays.
Hamerton's guardian authorized the purchase of the 'Spectator' in eight volumes.
Hamerton did not read any Scott novels until after reading his poems extensively.
Hamerton initially believed Scott's poems could not be surpassed by his novels.
Hamerton's father died when Hamerton was a child.
Hamerton experienced rapid mental growth in the two to three years after his father's death.
Hamerton developed a keen taste for literature after his father's death.
Hamerton found no enjoyment in Latin books at school.
Hamerton only gained access to the literary beauty of Latin much later through French and Italian.
Hamerton began Greek vigorously at Doncaster but did not get beyond the rudiments.
Hamerton wrote English verses very frequently from age eleven to twenty-one.
Hamerton believed writing English verses was a profitable exercise in language.
Hamerton's early verses were echoes of Sir Walter Scott.
Hamerton dreamed a new ballad about a shipwreck and wrote it down upon waking.
Hamerton's life at Doncaster was not on the whole unhappy.
Hamerton had lived under the tyranny of his father before attending Doncaster.
Hamerton found Mr. Cape's severity a light affliction compared to his father's tyranny.
Hamerton played a trick on Mr. Cape by hiding his friend Brokenribs in a copper.
Hamerton was a fag to a private pupil at some point.
Hamerton's private pupil only ordered him to light fires.
Hamerton learned to light fires quickly and well as a fag.
Hamerton still lit fires himself as an adult.
Hamerton believed every boy should be taught to do without servants on occasion.
Hamerton had an intense affection for dogs.
Hamerton begged Mr. Cape to keep a tawny dog at school.
Hamerton was rather a favorite with Mr. Cape.
Hamerton bought fresh straw for his dog and cared for it greatly.
Hamerton's dog became strongly attached to him.
Hamerton would have fought any boy his own age to protect his dog.
A private pupil cut off Hamerton's dog's tail despite his remonstrances.
Hamerton pitied his dog when it suffered with its bleeding stump.
Hamerton disliked balls intensely as an adult.
Hamerton would travel fifty miles to avoid attending a ball.
Hamerton believed Scott had the advantage of being almost always interesting.
Hamerton believed Scott never left an unhealthy feeling in the mind.
Hamerton was a favorite with the head-master at his school.
Hamerton brought a dog to school and kept it in a box in the wash-house.
Hamerton became strongly attached to his dog and cared for it with great affection.
Hamerton would have fought any boy his own age to defend his dog.
A private pupil cut off Hamerton's dog's tail despite his protests.
A private pupil shot Hamerton's dog in a field.
Hamerton knew what the private pupil was going to do but had no power to prevent it.
A school bully stole Hamerton's telescope and refused to return it.
Hamerton never received redress for the stolen telescope.
Hamerton was astonished by how some boys gained ascendency over others without obvious reasons.
Hamerton was born a boating creature.
Hamerton was not born to be a cricketing creature.
Hamerton lied to Mr. Cape about being able to swim.
Hamerton believed that man was naturally a swimming animal and that courage alone would enable him to swim.
Hamerton nearly drowned when he was thrown into the river Don while wearing a safety cord.
Hamerton made a firm resolution to learn to swim after his near-drowning experience.
Hamerton eventually became an expert swimmer through years of practice in France.
Hamerton could swim while reading a book aloud, swimming in full clothes with heavy boots, and towing a boat.
Hamerton attributed his perseverance in swimming to the shame he felt from his early misadventure.
Hamerton hated cricket from the very beginning.
Hamerton found cricket pure slavery and never desired to excel at it.
Hamerton's dislike of cricket caused him isolation as an English boy.
Hamerton was fond of ordinary walking and took long walks on half-holidays.
Hamerton escaped early on summer mornings to walk around the race-course.
Hamerton occasionally had headaches.
Hamerton's doctor was a kind friend who invited him to his house.
Hamerton fought with the doctor's son many times because elder boys set them on.
Hamerton was extremely religious during his time at Doncaster.
Hamerton had a firm belief in providential interferences in his life, even in trifling matters.
Hamerton had a theological turn of mind.
Hamerton sometimes inserted passages of his own into sermon reports.
Hamerton once produced a complete original sermon instead of reporting the preacher's sermon.
Hamerton's original sermon cost him a reprimand but excited the interest of the master.
Hamerton was obliged to take notes of sermons and write them out in abridged form at Doncaster.
Hamerton's prayers had special reference to a country house belonging to an old lady.
The old lady was grandmother to a friend of Hamerton's and extended grandmotherly kindness to him.
Hamerton learned that courage alone is not enough to enable swimming without acquired skill.
Hamerton taught his sons to swim as an art, just as one learns to skate.
Hamerton believed there is nothing better for a boy than to be roused to perseverance through shame.
Hamerton felt no shame about not being able to play cricket well.
Hamerton had a theological turn of mind as a boy.
Hamerton sometimes inserted his own passages into sermon notes at Doncaster School.
Hamerton once produced a complete original sermon based on a text, which earned him a reprimand.
Hamerton was a firm believer in Christianity as a boy but did not belong to any party in the Church of England.
Hamerton believed a boy's religion should be quiet and practical with simple, uncontroversial theology.
Hamerton spent three half-years at Doncaster School.
Mr. Cape thrashed Hamerton many times with his cane.
Hamerton felt grief when Mr. Cape died, despite the physical punishment he had received.
Hamerton had no natural bent for mathematics.
Hamerton performed mathematics tasks in a perfunctory manner.
Hamerton was a well-educated boy at age thirteen.
Hamerton had fallen behind in his education by age fifteen or sixteen.
Hamerton had a strong affection for his guardian.
Hamerton learned his guardian had an incurable heart disease that could cause sudden death.
After learning of his guardian's heart disease, Hamerton resolved to stay near her as much as possible.
Hamerton remained at Burnley for several years to stay near his guardian.
Hamerton's decision to remain at Burnley made him an indifferent classical scholar.
Hamerton had a great affection for Dr. Butler, his headmaster at Burnley Grammar School.
Dr. Butler never could bring himself to say anything disagreeable to Hamerton.
Hamerton worked at French regularly at Burnley School.
Hamerton learned just enough German to read one thin volume.
Hamerton had a natural taste for French and Italian but not for German.
Hamerton acquired elementary knowledge of chemistry at Burnley School.
Hamerton's chemistry knowledge influenced his later attention to etching.
Hamerton etched his first plate as a boy at Burnley School, a portrait of a Jew with a turban.
Hamerton's first etching was frightfully over-bitten.
Hamerton wrote English essays at Burnley School under Dr. Butler's encouragement.
Hamerton wrote verses almost without intermission between the ages of twelve and twenty-one.
Hamerton won a prize at Burnley School in 1847 for a poem on Prince Charles Edward after the Battle of Culloden.
Hamerton was not yet thirteen years old when he won the poetry prize.
Hamerton's boyish poetry showed the influence of Virgil, Scott, and Byron.
Hamerton translated many of the Odes of Horace into English verse under Dr. Butler's encouragement.
Hamerton did not preserve any of his Horace translations.
Hamerton participated in public discussions on historical subjects at Burnley School.
Hamerton was chosen to defend Queen Elizabeth's character in a school debate.
Hamerton spoke for an hour and a half in the debate on Queen Elizabeth.
Hamerton's hair was red when he was a boy at Burnley School.
Hamerton's hair darkened as he grew older.
Hamerton had a love of boating as a boy.
Hamerton had always loved old-world surroundings with carved oak, tapestry, and stained glass.
Hamerton was in danger of coveting the Holme, a country house that had belonged to Dr. Whitaker.
